<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008488 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00032: (2026x Refresh1) Libraries

- page_id: `304008488`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008488/2026x+Refresh1+Libraries
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:17:58.632+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can import multiple libraries in the Systems Cybersecurity Designer plugin instead of creating elements. All imported libraries are treated as used projects in your current project.

##### Adding a library to the project

To add a library to the project

- In the Containment tree, right-click Model> Security and then select the required library. [ATTACHMENT filename='Add Library.png']

If you click on any element in the library, you will be redirected to the web page of the respective element.

##### MITRE CWE Library

The addition of the MITRE CWE Library adds a package to the Containment tree. The MITRE CWE Librarycontains two sub-packages: Common Weakness Enumeration and Functional Cybersecurity Requirements. The Common Weakness Enumeration package contains a list of weaknesses. Each weakness contains a hyperlink that redirects you to the CWE definition web page. The Functional Cybersecurity Requirements package contains the requirements to mitigate the weakness. These requirements are segregated with the help of sub-packages based on the type of requirement.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']MITRE Library present in the Containment tree.

##### MITRE ATT&CK EnterpriseTechnique Library

The addition of MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Technique Library adds two packages to the Containment tree: MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Technique Library and MITRE ATT&CK Platforms Library. The MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Technique Library package contains sub-packages which contain enterprise mitigation, tactics and techniques. The MITRE ATT&CK Platforms Library contains the platforms through which the attacks can take place.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Technique Library present in the Containment tree.

##### MITRE ATT&CK ICSTechnique Library

The addition of MITRE ATT&CK ICS Technique Library adds a package to the Containment tree. The MITRE ATT&CK ICS Technique Library package contains sub-packages which contain ICS mitigation, tactics and techniques.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### MITRE ATT&CK ICSTechnique Library present in the Containment tree.

##### NIST Control Library

The addition of the NIST Control Library adds a package to the Containment tree. The NIST Control Library package contains a sub-package that lists the cyber security requirements.[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### NIST Control Library present in the Containment tree.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">MITRE CWE Library </span></span>contains two sub-packages: Common Weakness Enumeration and Functional Cybersecurity Requirements.  The Common Weakness Enumeration package contains a list of weaknesses. Each weakness contains a hyperlink that redirects you to the CWE definition web page. The Functional Cybersecurity Requirements package contains the requirements to mitigate the weakness. These requirements are segregated with the help of sub-packages based on the type of requirement.</span></p><p><br /></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="MITRE CWE Library.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Libraries" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>MITRE Library present in the Containment tree.</h6><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">MITRE ATT&amp;CK Enterprise </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Technique Library</span></h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The addition of MITRE ATT&amp;CK Enterprise Technique Library adds two packages to the Containment tree: MITRE ATT&amp;CK Enterprise Technique Library and MITRE ATT&amp;CK Platforms Library. The MITRE ATT&amp;CK Enterprise Technique Library package contains sub-packages which contain enterprise mitigation, tactics and techniques. The MITRE ATT&amp;CK Platforms Library contains the platforms through which the attacks can take place.<br /><br /></span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Enterprise_Library.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Libraries" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">MITRE ATT&amp;CK Enterprise Technique Library present in the Containment tree.</h6><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">MITRE ATT&amp;CK ICS </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Technique Library</span></h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The addition of MITRE ATT&amp;CK ICS Technique Library adds a package to the Containment tree. The MITRE ATT&amp;CK ICS Technique Library package contains sub-packages which contain ICS mitigation, tactics and techniques.<br /><br /></span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ICS_Library.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Libraries" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">MITRE ATT&amp;CK ICSTechnique Library present in the Containment tree.</h6><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">NIST Control Library</span></h3><p style="text-align: left;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The addition of the NIST Control Library adds a package to the Containment tree. The NIST Control Library package contains a sub-package that lists the c</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">yber security requirements.<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="NIST Library.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Libraries" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">NIST Control Library present in the Containment tree.</h6>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304009005 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00033: (2026x Refresh1) Manual Attack Path

- page_id: `304009005`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304009005/2026x+Refresh1+Manual+Attack+Path
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:19.332+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > (2026x Refresh1) Table > (2026x Refresh1) Threat  scenario
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Attack Path**

A set of deliberate actions to realize a threat scenario.

**Attack Path Steps**

Enumerate each step needed to perform the threat scenario.

**Attack Feasibility Rating**

An attribute of an attack path describes the ease of successfully carrying out the corresponding set of actions. By default, the ISO/SAE 21434:2021 rating scale is used.

##### Creating a Manual Attack Path

A ManualAttack Path refers to the manual way of describing and rating the feasibility of attack paths. It does not refer to potential based attacks or CVSS methodologies.

To create a ManualAttack Path

1. In the Containment tree, right-click**Attack Paths and Feasibility Ratings**and select Create Element. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**and select **ManualAttack Path**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword attack and then select **Manual** Attack Path. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Name the created Manual Attack Path in the Containment tree and press Enter. The ManualAttack Path has the prefix **AP**,which denotes that the created element is a Manual Attack Path; the number**1** indicates that it is the first Manual Attack Path created. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

##### Creating a Manual Attack Path Table

If you create a new project using the**ISO 21434 Project**template, then a**Manual Attack Paths**tablealready exists in the**1.3 Attack Paths and Feasibility Ratings**package.

To create aManual Attack PathTable

1. In the Containment tree, right-click **Attack Paths and Feasibility Ratings** and select Create Diagram. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**and select **Manual Attack** **Path Table**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword attack and then select **Manual Attack Path** Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Manual Attack Table is displayed in the diagram pane of the modeling tool. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding a Manual Attack Path to Manual Attack Path Table

To add a new Manual Attack Path to the Manual Attack Path Table

1. In the Manual Attack Path Table, click Add New. A row is added in the Manual Attack Path Table, which shows the new Manual Attack Path. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. In the newly created Manual Attack Path's row and the**Name**column, double-click the designated cell to name the Manual Attack Path. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

To add an existing Manual Attack Path to the Manual Attack Path Table

1. In the Manual Attack Path Table, click Add Existing. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Attack Path** dialog, select the required Manual Attack Path. A row is added to the Manual Attack Path Table, which shows the existing Manual Attack Path. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. In the existing Manual Attack Path's row and the**Name**column, double-click the designated cell to rename the Manual Attack Path. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

##### Adding Attack Path Steps

To add Attack Path Steps to the Manual Attack Path Table

1. Double-click the designated cell in the **Attack Path Steps** column and the required Manual Attack Path's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] . [ATTACHMENT filename='Adding attack path_Three dot.png']
2. From the **Select Situation******dialog, select Attack Path Steps. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The Attack Path Steps now shown in the Manual Attack Path Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- You can also drag and drop the Attack Path Step from the Containment tree to the Threat Scenario Table.
- You can move the Attack Path Steps in the Select Situation dialog by clicking Up or Down . [ATTACHMENT filename='Movement of attack paths.png']

##### Adding an Attack Feasibility Rating

To rate the Attack Feasibility

- Double-click the cell in the Manual Attack Path's row and from the drop-down list, select Attack Feasibility Rating. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The Attack Feasibility Rating is now shown in the Manual Attack Path Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Manual Attack Path Table Example

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### INFO: References

References

[ISO/SAE 21434:2021 Road vehicles-Cybersecurity enginee](https://www.iso.org/obp/ui/fr/)r

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Basic Concepts</h3><p><strong>Attack Path</strong></p><p>A set of deliberate actions to realize a threat scenario.</p><p><strong>Attack Path Steps</strong></p><p>Enumerate each step needed to perform the threat scenario.</p><p><strong>Attack Feasibility Rating</strong></p><p>An attribute of an attack path describes the ease of successfully carrying out the corresponding set of actions. By default, the ISO/SAE 21434:2021 rating scale is used.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Creating a Manual <span>Attack Path</span></h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33960afa-9338-4985-843d-537893e4b919"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">A <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Manual </span>Attack Path refers to the manual way of describing and rating the feasibility of attack paths. It does not refer to potential based attacks or CVSS methodologies.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To create a <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Manual </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Attack Path</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Containment tree, right-click<span> </span><strong>Attack Paths and Feasibility Ratings</strong><span> </span>and select</span><strong style="text-align: left;"><span> </span>Create Element.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Create Element.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Manual Attack Path" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Do one of the following:</span><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the dialog, expand <strong>ISO 21434 </strong>and select <strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Manual </span>Attack Path</strong>.<br /></span><strong><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Manual Attack Path_Menu.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Manual Attack Path" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li><li class="auto-cursor-target">In the search tab, type the keyword attack and then select<span> <strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Manual </span></strong></span><strong>Attack Path.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Manual Attack Path_Menu_Search.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Manual Attack Path" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li></ul></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Name the created Manual Attack Path in the Containment tree and press Enter. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008814 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00034: (2026x Refresh1) Misuse Cases

- page_id: `304008814`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008814/2026x+Refresh1+Misuse+Cases
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:08.167+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > (2026x Refresh1) Pre Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Misuse Cases**

Unintended actions (according to the design intent) undertaken by a person or system to interact with systems, interfaces, or data.

##### Adding Misuse Cases

To add a misuse case

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the 01 -Misuse Cases package and select Create Element. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. I n the dialog, expand **DO-326A** and select **Misuse Case**. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
3. Name the created misuse case in the Containment tree. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

You can add the newly created Misuse Case to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Basic Concepts</h3><p><strong>Misuse Cases</strong></p><p>Unintended actions (according to the design intent) undertaken by a person or system to interact with systems, interfaces, or data.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5a4d315a-d27a-4ac9-be1d-32b2154876ea">Adding Misuse Cases</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p>To add a misuse case</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment tree, right-click the <strong>01 -Misuse Cases </strong><span>package and select</span><strong> Create Element.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Create Element.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Misuse Cases" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li><li>I<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">n the dialog, expand <strong>DO-326A</strong> and select <strong>Misuse Case</strong>.<br /><br /><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Misuse Case selection.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Misuse Cases" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong><br /><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Name the created misuse case in the Containment tree.<br /><br /><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Misuse Case created.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Misuse Cases" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong></span></li></ol><p>You can add the newly created Misuse Case to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008989 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00035: (2026x Refresh1) Operational Situation

- page_id: `304008989`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008989/2026x+Refresh1+Operational+Situation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:18.144+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > (2026x Refresh1) Table > (2026x Refresh1) Damage scenario
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Creating an Operational Situation

To create an Operational Situation

1. In the Containment tree, right-click**Damage Scenario****and Impact Ratings**and select Create Element. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**and select **Operational Situation**. **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
  - In the search tab, type the keyword operational and then select Operational Situation. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
3. Name the created Operational Situation in the Containment tree and press Enter. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

##### Adding Operational Situation to the Operational Situations Table

Using the Operational SituationsTable you can only add nested Operational Situations.

To add an Operational Situation

1. In the Operational SituationsTable, select an Operational Situationand then click Add Nested. A row is added in the Operational SituationsTable, which shows the nested Operational Situation. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. In the newly created Operational Situation'srow and the **Name**column, double-click the designated cell to name the Effect. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding Operational Situation's parameters to the Operational Situations Table

To add Operational Situation's parameters to the Operational Situations Table

1. Double-click the designated cell in the**Vehicle Usage** column and the required Operational Situation's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Elements******dialog, select the Vehicle Usage. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The Vehicle Usage is added to the Operational Situations Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
All other Operational Situation parameters are added to the Operational Situations Table using the same procedure. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

/><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Operational Situation_Select Elements dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Operational Situation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br />The Vehicle Usage is added to the Operational Situations Table.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Operational Situation_Parameters_added.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Operational Situation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br style="text-align: left;" />All other Operational Situation parameters are added to the Operational Situations Table using the same procedure.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Operational Situation_Parameters_added_all.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Operational Situation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></span></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008472 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00036: (2026x Refresh1) Post Risk Assessment

- page_id: `304008472`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008472/2026x+Refresh1+Post+Risk+Assessment
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:17:58.181+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This phase builds directly on the results of the pre-risk assessment. The Post-Threat Scenario is a refined version of the Pre-Threat Scenario, updated to reflect the effectiveness of the implemented security measures. The Effectiveness Method is applied to evaluate how well these measures reduce the identified risks. Attack Paths are also refined to show how mitigations change the feasibility or likelihood of attacks. Finally, specific Security Measures are defined and validated to ensure that the system risk is mitigated.

To learn more, refer to the following pages:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This phase builds directly on the results of the pre-risk assessment. The Post-Threat Scenario is a refined version of the Pre-Threat Scenario, updated to reflect the effectiveness of the implemented security measures. The Effectiveness Method is applied to evaluate how well these measures reduce the identified risks. Attack Paths are also refined to show how mitigations change the feasibility or likelihood of attacks. Finally, specific Security Measures are defined and validated to ensure that the system risk is mitigated.</p><p>To learn more<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">, refer to the following pages:</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5e036bc4-6958-4320-a95a-9e0158a0d2d3" /></span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008896 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00037: (2026x Refresh1) Post-Threat Scenario Table

- page_id: `304008896`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008896/2026x+Refresh1+Post-Threat+Scenario+Table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:11.989+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > (2026x Refresh1) Post Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Opening the Post-Threat Scenario Table

To open the Post-Threat Scenario Table

- In the Containment tree, expand the 01 - Post-Threat Scenarios package and open the 01 - Post-Threat Scenarios table. [ATTACHMENT filename='Post Risk Assessment Table.png']

The **01 - Post-Threat Scenarios** table displays the Higher Level Threat Scenario you created in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table' space=''] table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding a Threat Scenario

To add a new Threat Scenario to the Post-Threat Scenario Table

1. In the Post-Threat Scenarios table , click Add New to add a new Threat Scenario. [ATTACHMENT filename='Add New.png']
2. In the newly created Threat Scenario's row and the Name column, double-click the designated cell to name the Threat Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To add an existing Threat Scenario to the Post-Threat Scenario Table

1. In the Post-Threat Scenario Table , click Add Existing. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Threat Scenario** dialog, select the required Threat Scenario. A row is added to the Post-Threat Scenario Table, showing the existing Threat Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. In the existing Threat Scenario's row and the **Name**column, double-click the designated cell to rename the Threat Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding Documentation

To add documentation

- In the Threat Scenario's row and the **Documentation** column, double-click the designated cell and type the required data.

##### Adding a Threat Source, Vulnerability, and Misuse Case

To add a Threat Source

1. Double-click the designated cell in the **Threat Source** column and the required Threat Scenario's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Elements******dialog, select Threat Source and click **OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The Threat Source is added to the Post-Threat Scenario Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Similarly, add the **Vulnerability**and **Misuse Case**to the Post-Threat Scenario Table.

##### Adding a Security Measure, Attack Path, and Requirement

To add a Security Measure, Attack Path, and Requirement

1. Double-click the designated cell in the **Security Measure** column and the required Threat Scenario's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Elements******dialog, select one or more Security Measure(s) and click **OK**. 
 
*[IMAGE alt='' src='']* 
 
The Security Measure(s) are added to the Post-Threat Scenario Table. 
 
*[IMAGE alt='' src='']*
3. Similarly, add the Attack Path and Requirement to the Post-Threat Scenario Table.

The**Aggregated Impact Rating, Effectiveness, Likelihood, and Risk** values are calculated automatically based on the [DO-326A standards](https://products.rtca.org/21dja1f/).

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008470 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00038: (2026x Refresh1) Pre Risk Assessment

- page_id: `304008470`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008470/2026x+Refresh1+Pre+Risk+Assessment
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:17:58.113+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

At this stage, the system is analyzed for potential cybersecurity threats. This involves identifying Misuse Cases (how the system could be intentionally misused), constructing a Pre-Threat Scenario Table to list possible threats, defining Attack Paths from an IBD that an attacker might exploit, and documenting known Vulnerabilities. Based on this information, initial requirements are added to guide future mitigation, and potential threat sources are identified and will later lead to a POST-Threat Scenario Table.

To learn more, refer to the following pages:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>At this stage, the system is analyzed for potential cybersecurity threats. This involves identifying Misuse Cases (how the system could be intentionally misused), constructing a Pre-Threat Scenario Table to list possible threats, defining Attack Paths from an IBD that an attacker might exploit, and documenting known Vulnerabilities. Based on this information, initial requirements are added to guide future mitigation, and potential threat sources are identified and will later lead to a POST-Threat Scenario Table.</p><p>To learn more<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">, refer to the following pages:</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5e036bc4-6958-4320-a95a-9e0158a0d2d3" /></span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008820 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00039: (2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table

- page_id: `304008820`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008820/2026x+Refresh1+Pre+Threat+Scenario+Table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:08.557+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > (2026x Refresh1) Pre Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Threat Scenario**

The specification of intentional unauthorized electronic interaction, consisting of a contributing threat source (attacker and attack path), vulnerabilities, operational conditions, resulting threat conditions, and events by which the target was attacked.

##### Opening the Pre-Threat Scenario Table

To open the Pre-Threat Scenario Table

- In the Containment tree, expand the 02 - Pre-Threat Scenarios package and open the 02 - Pre-Threat Scenarios table. [ATTACHMENT filename='Existing Table.png']

##### Adding a Threat Scenario

To add a new Threat Scenario to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table

1. In the Pre-Threat Scenarios table , click Add New to add a new Threat Scenario. [ATTACHMENT filename='Add New.png']
2. In the newly created Threat Scenario's row and the Name column, double-click the designated cell to name the Threat Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To add an existing Threat Scenario to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table

1. In the Pre-Threat Scenario Table , click Add Existing. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Threat Scenario** dialog, select the required Threat Scenario. A row is added to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table, showing the existing Threat Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. In the existing Threat Scenario's row and the **Name**column, double-click the designated cell to rename the Threat Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding Documentation

To add documentation

- In the Threat Scenario's row and the **Documentation** column, double-click the designated cell and type the required data.

##### Adding Threat Source, Vulnerability, and Misuse Case

To add a Threat Source

1. Double-click the designated cell in the **Threat Source** column and the required Threat Scenario's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Elements******dialog, select Threat Source and click **OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The Threat Source is added to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Similarly, add the **Vulnerability**and **Misuse Case**to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.

##### Adding a Threat Condition, Attack Path, and Requirement

To add a Threat Condition, Attack Path, and Requirement

1. Double-click the designated cell in the **Threat Condition** column and the required Threat Scenario's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Elements******dialog, select one or more Threat Condition(s) and click **OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The Threat Condition(s) are added to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Similarly, add the Attack Path and Requirement to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.

The following table outlines the Severity of the Threat Condition Effect. The table represents the Acceptable, Negotiable and Unacceptable risk based upon the Level of Threat.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding a Lower-Level Threat Scenario

To add a Lower Level Threat Scenario

1. In the**02 - Pre Threat Scenarios** package, right-click a Threat Scenario and select **Security>Add Lower Level Threat Scenario**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the Select Destination Package dialog, select the required package and click **OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Name the newly created Lower Level Threat Scenario under the 01 - Post Threat Scenarios package in the Containment tree.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Table toolbars' space='MT'] the Pre-Threat Scenario Table to view the newly added Lower Level Threat Scenario in the table. [ATTACHMENT filename='Lower Level TS.png']

The **Aggregated Impact Rating, Likelihood, and Risk** values are calculated automatically based on the [DO-326A standards](https://products.rtca.org/21dja1f/).

The numerical values are added together to calculate the Likelihood Rating. The formula below is used to calculate the Likelihood Rating.

- Likelihood Rating = Elapsed Time + Specialist Expertise+ Knowledge of Item/Component+ Window of Opportunity+ Equipment

The following table shows the numerical values assigned to the Likelihood. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

intentional unauthorized electronic interaction, consisting of a contributing threat source (attacker and attack path), vulnerabilities, operational conditions, resulting threat conditions, and events by which the target was attacked.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Opening the Pre-Threat Scenario Table</h3><p>To open the Pre-Threat style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="15"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Three Dot _Icon.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Threat Source_Edit button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">From the <strong>Select Elements</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, select Threat Source and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select Element dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br />The Threat Source is added to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Threat Source_added.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Similarly, add the <strong>Vulnerability </strong>and <strong>Misuse Case </strong>to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.</span></li></ol><h3>Adding a Threat Condition, Attack Path, and Requirement</h3><p>To add a Threat Condition, Attack Path, and Requirement</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Double-click the designated cell in the <strong>Threat Condition </strong></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">column and the required Threat Scenario's row and cl<span>ick <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size" style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="15"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Three Dot _Icon.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Threat Condition_Edit.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">From the <strong>Select Elements</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, select one or more Threat Condition(s) and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select Element dialog_Threat Conditions.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br />The Threat Condition(s) are added to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Threat Condition_added.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li>Similarly, add the <strong>Attack Path </strong><span>and </span><strong>Requirement </strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.</span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3cb76936-558b-4eb5-ae45-86d37a21bbec"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following table outlines the Severity of the Threat Condition Effect. The table represents the Acceptable, Negotiable and Unacceptable risk based upon the Level of Threat.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Matrix.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Adding a Lower-Level Threat Scenario</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To add a Lower Level Threat Scenario</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the<strong> 02 - Pre Threat Scenarios</strong> package, right-click a Threat Scenario and select <strong>Security&gt;Add Lower Level Threat Scenario</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Lower Level TS_Command.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li data-uuid="9aba587d-ac8d-4218-8f5f-48f3a3236db4"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">From the Select Destination Package dialog, select the required package and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select destination package.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li data-uuid="8714495b-8a0d-4736-a1d5-8766dd3fd1de"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Name the newly created </span>Lower Level Threat Scenario under the <strong>01 - Post Threat Scenarios</strong> package in the Containment tree.</li><li data-uuid="f5763a69-5ac0-4520-a816-74d8aec54cc9"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Table toolbars" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Refresh ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>the Pre-Threat Scenario Table to view the newly added Lower Level Threat Scenario in the table.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Lower Level TS.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5436f069-73df-4129-a02a-9736d2d52c07"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>Aggregated Impact Rating, Likelihood, and Risk</strong> values are calculated automatically based on the <a href="https://products.rtca.org/21dja1f/">DO-326A standards</a>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ed50b00c-7036-4eef-bb94-12d236c84149"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The numerical values are added together to calculate the Likelihood Rating. The formula below is used to calculate the Likelihood Rating.</p><ul><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Likelihood Rating = Elapsed Time + Specialist Expertise+ Knowledge of Item/Component+ Window of Opportunity+ Equipment</span></li></ul><p><span>The following table shows the numerical values assigned to the Likelihood.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Likelihood.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Pre Threat Scenario Table" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008933 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00040: (2026x Refresh1) Refined Attack Paths

- page_id: `304008933`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008933/2026x+Refresh1+Refined+Attack+Paths
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:14.020+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > (2026x Refresh1) Post Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The refined attack path is used in the post-threat risk assessment study to create an attack path that includes security functions.

##### Refining a Lower-Level Attack Path

To refine a Lower-Level Attack Path

1. In the**03 - Attack Paths**package, right-click an Attack Path and select **Security>Add Lower Level Attack Path**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the Select Destination Package dialog, select the required package and click **OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Name the newly created Lower-Level Attack Path under the **02 - Attack Paths** package in the Containment tree.
4. Modify the newly created attack path as per your requirement. To learn more about modifying an attack path, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Attack Paths' space=''] page. [ATTACHMENT filename='RefinedAP.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The refined attack path is used in the post-threat risk assessment study to create an attack path that includes security functions.</p><h3 style="text-align: left;">Refining a Lower-Level Attack Path</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To refine a Lower-Level Attack Path</span></p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="b9ed5f0d-bd15-40b1-908b-1d9c7736c34e"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">In the<strong><span> 03 - Attack Paths</span></strong><span> </span>package, right-click an Attack Path and select <strong>Security&gt;Add Lower Level Attack Path</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Add lower level attack path.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Refined Attack Paths" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li data-uuid="8a6fba86-8471-47be-8692-e889d35eceb0"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">From the Select Destination Package dialog, select the required package and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select destination package dialog.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Refined Attack Paths" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li data-uuid="1411f6f6-8149-44b1-baa3-4bba5b4b1f09"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Name the newly created<span> </span></span>Lower-Level Attack Path under the<span> <strong>02 - Attack Paths</strong></span><span> </span>package in the Containment tree.</li><li data-uuid="89b47735-b88b-4cd8-91cf-751fc83df092">Modify the newly created attack path as per your requirement. To learn more about modifying an attack path, refer to the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Attack Paths" /></ac:link> page.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="RefinedAP.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Refined Attack Paths" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008877 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00041: (2026x Refresh1) Requirements

- page_id: `304008877`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008877/2026x+Refresh1+Requirements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:10.885+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > (2026x Refresh1) Pre Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Requirement**

An identifiable element of a function specification that can be validated and against which an implementation can be verified.

##### Adding Requirements

To add requirements

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the 05 -Requirements package and select Create Element. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - I n the dialog, expand **General** and select **Requirement**. [ATTACHMENT filename='Requirement selection.png']
  - In the search tab, type the requirement and then select **Requirement** . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Name the newly created requirement in the Containment tree. The prefix 1 denotes the requirement count. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can add the newly created Requirement to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Basic Concepts</h3><p><strong>Requirement</strong></p><p>An identifiable element of a function specification that can be validated and against which an implementation can be verified.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Adding Requirements</h3><p>To add requirements</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment tree, right-click the <strong>05 -Requirements </strong><span>package and select</span><strong> Create Element.<br /><br /></strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Create Element.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Requirements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li class="auto-cursor-target">Do one of the following:<ul><li>I<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">n the dialog, expand <strong>General</strong> and select <strong>Requirement</strong>.</span><strong><br /><br /></strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Requirement selection.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Requirements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li class="auto-cursor-target">In the search tab, type the requirement and then select <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>Requirement</strong></span><strong>.<br /></strong><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Requirement search.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Requirements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></li></ul></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Name the newly created requirement in the Containment tree. The prefix 1 denotes the requirement count.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Requirement created.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Requirements" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></span></li></ol><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">You can add the newly created Requirement to the Pre-Threat Scenario Table.</span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304009130 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00042: (2026x Refresh1) Risk Computation Customization

- page_id: `304009130`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304009130/2026x+Refresh1+Risk+Computation+Customization
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:27.101+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > (2026x Refresh1) Table > (2026x Refresh1) TARA
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To customize the Risk Computation

1. Create a new project and profile:
  1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a new project' space='MT'] .
  2. Select Options>Project Usages , and add the ISO 21434 Profile.mdzip file to it.
  3. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Profiles' space='UPDG'] under the root package.
  4. Provide the required name, for example, My Customized ISO21434 Profile .
  5. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Profile diagram' space='MT'] in the My Customized ISO21434 Profile.
  6. Provide the required name, for example, My Customized Threat Scenario .
2. Create a new customization:
  1. Search for ThreatScenarioCustomization available in the ISO 21434 Profile, and drag and drop it into the profile diagram.
  2. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating your First Customization' space='UPDG'] .
  3. Provide the required name, for example, MyThreatScenarioCustomization .
  4. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generalization' space='MT'] relationship between the newly created customization element and ThreatScenarioCustomization .
  5. Using the Specification window of MyThreatScenarioCustomization, assign the value to the Customization Target property as ThreatScenario.
3. Create Customization Element Properties:
  1. Select the customization element, open the smart manipulator, and select the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and specifying derived properties' space='UPDG'] Specification.
  2. Provide the required name for example SafetyRiskValue.
  3. Using the Specification window, assign:
    1. Type: Real [UML Primitive Types]
    2. Multiplicity: [0.1]
    3. Redefined Property: +SafetyRiskValue (From the ISO 21434 profile)
4. Create an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining expressions' space='UPDG'] for the Derived property:
  1. In the Specification window of the derived property (SafetyRiskValue), launch the editor for the Expression property.
  2. Select Create operation and then select the Real value.
  3. Provide the required Name and Value.
  4. Share the created profile and save the project.

Using the same steps, create other required derived properties and create required expressions for them (Operational, Financial, and Privacy Risk Value).

Once you add the created project containing the customized profile, the customized risk values are visible in any TARA project containing the threat scenario elements.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To customize the Risk Computation</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a new project and profile:<ol><li>Create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Creating a new project" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[new UML project]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Select <strong>Options&gt;Project Usages</strong>, and add the <strong>ISO 21434 Profile.mdzip</strong> file to it.</li><li>Create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDG" ri:content-title="Creating Profiles" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[new profile]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> under the root package.</li><li>Provide the required name, for example, <strong>My Customized ISO21434 Profile</strong>.</li><li>Create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Profile diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[new profile diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the My Customized ISO21434 Profile.</li><li>Provide the required name, for example, <strong>My Customized Threat Scenario</strong>.</li></ol></li><li>Create a new customization:<ol><li>Search for <strong>ThreatScenarioCustomization </strong>available in the ISO 21434 Profile, and drag and drop it into the profile diagram.</li><li>Create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDG" ri:content-title="Creating your First Customization" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[new customization element]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Provide the required name, for example, <strong>MyThreatScenarioCustomization</strong>.</li><li>Create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Generalization" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[generalization ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>relationship between the newly created customization element and <strong>ThreatScenarioCustomization</strong>.</li><li>Using the Specification window of MyThreatScenarioCustomization, assign the value to the Customization Target property as ThreatScenario.</li></ol></li><li>Create Customization Element Properties:<ol><li>Select the customization element, open the smart manipulator, and select the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDG" ri:content-title="Creating and specifying derived properties" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Derived Property]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> Specification.</li><li>Provide the required name for example SafetyRiskValue.</li><li>Using the Specification window, assign:<ol><li><p>Type: Real [UML Primitive Types]</p></li><li>Multiplicity: [0.1]</li><li>Redefined Property: +SafetyRiskValue (From the ISO 21434 profile)</li></ol></li></ol></li><li>Create an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDG" ri:content-title="Defining expressions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[expression]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the Derived property:<ol><li>In the Specification window of the derived property (SafetyRiskValue), launch the editor for the Expression property.</li><li>Select Create operation and then select the <strong>Real </strong>value.</li><li>Provide the required Name and Value.</li><li>Share the created profile and save the project.</li></ol></li></ol><p>Using the same steps, create other required derived properties and create required expressions for them (Operational, Financial, and Privacy Risk Value).</p><p>Once you add the created project containing the customized profile, the customized risk values are visible in any TARA project containing the threat scenario elements.</p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008927 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00043: (2026x Refresh1) Security Measures

- page_id: `304008927`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008927/2026x+Refresh1+Security+Measures
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:13.511+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > (2026x Refresh1) Post Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Security measures**

Used to mitigate or control a threat condition. Security measures may be features, functions, or procedures, both onboard or offboard.

##### Identifying an L3-level security function as a functional security measure

To identify an L3-level security function as a functional security measure

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an L3-level security function and click Stereotype . [ATTACHMENT filename='Select_Stereotype.png']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, select**FunctionalSecurityMeasure[Class]**and click **Apply.** [ATTACHMENT filename='Select_Funtional Security Measure.png']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword security, select **FunctionalSecurityMeasure[Class],** and click **Apply**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The L3 level security function is defined as a functional security measure in the Containment tree. You can also assign the Stereotype to any L3 level security function with the aid of the Specification dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### L3 level security function defined as a functional security measure.

- You must drag and drop the security measures in the L3 Internal Block Diagram to use them in the architecture.
- Security Measures are highlighted in Green when it is selected as an Attack Path Step.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Basic Concepts</h3><p><strong>Security measures</strong></p><p>Used to mitigate or control a threat condition. Security measures may be features, functions, or procedures, both onboard or offboard.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Identifying an L3-level security function as a functional security measure</h3><p>To identify an L3-level security function as a functional security measure</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="b4f13383-3107-4204-836d-d1e585101471">In the Containment tree, right-click an L3-level security function and click <strong>Stereotype</strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select_Stereotype.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Security Measures" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li data-uuid="b8ae3d75-048c-454d-ba29-0e29f6bbc8a3"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Do one of the following:</span><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the dialog, select<span> <strong>FunctionalSecurityMeasure[Class] </strong>and click <strong>Apply.<br /><br /></strong></span></span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select_Funtional Security Measure.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Security Measures" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li class="auto-cursor-target">In the search tab, type the keyword security, select<span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>FunctionalSecurityMeasure[Class],</strong></span></span><strong> </strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">and click <strong>Apply</strong>.<br /></span><strong><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Search_Funtional Security Measure.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Security Measures" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li></ul></li></ol><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="0d7dabc0-165d-4ba1-af4e-849a039b76e0">The L3 level security function is defined as a functional security measure in the Containment tree. You can also assign the Stereotype to any L3 level security function with the aid of the Specification dialog.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Functional Security Defined.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Security Measures" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">L3 level security function defined as a functional security measure.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5fbd251d-598f-420d-9dec-3a7ee9387d1c"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">You must drag and drop the security measures in the L3 Internal Block Diagram to use them in the architecture.</span></li><li data-uuid="931d2269-587a-405d-88cc-60938f4d4918"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Security Measures are highlighted in Green when it is selected as an Attack Path Step.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008391 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00044: (2026x Refresh1) Security Scope

- page_id: `304008391`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008391/2026x+Refresh1+Security+Scope
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:17:56.880+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > (2026x Refresh1) Impact Analysis
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Assets are the logical and physical resources of the aircraft that contribute to the airworthiness of the aircraft. The purpose of the Security Scope Definition is to identify the assets, document the points of entry to the assets, and determine their environment.

The Security Scope Definition outputs are used in two ways, as part of the:

- Airworthiness Security Process to derive architectural and design constraints and requirements; and
- Continuing Airworthiness Process (documented in DO-355/ED-204), to derive Operator Guidance for the safe operation and maintenance of the

The Security Scope Definition phase is comprised of two parts:

- Security Perimeter - the notional boundary between an internal security context and the external security environment of the aircraft or system under consideration. It marks the change of security control. The internal security context is comprised of the assets contained in the security perimeter and the relevant security

Security Environment: the description of everything outside the security perimeter that is relevant to the security of the aircraft or system under consideration. The definition of security environments is equivalent to the *external context*as used in ISO 27005:2011.

To learn more about specifying security scope, refer to the following pages:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Assets are the logical and physical resources of the aircraft that contribute to the airworthiness of the aircraft. The purpose of the Security Scope Definition is to identify the assets, document the points of entry to the assets, and determine their environment.</p><p>The Security Scope Definition outputs are used in two ways, as part of the:</p><ul><li data-uuid="ab62b0c3-1fab-4efb-9648-a5ee02142391">Airworthiness Security Process to derive architectural and design constraints and requirements; and</li><li data-uuid="3639db67-eb57-4a1c-ab81-b315f148c05e">Continuing Airworthiness Process (documented in DO-355/ED-204), to derive Operator Guidance for the safe operation and maintenance of the</li></ul><p>The Security Scope Definition phase is comprised of two parts:</p><ul><li data-uuid="1214dd02-0f2e-4d6f-92c2-cb3818cf2a43">Security Perimeter - the notional boundary between an internal security context and the external security environment of the aircraft or system under consideration. It marks the change of security control. The internal security context is comprised of the assets contained in the security perimeter and the relevant security</li></ul><p>Security Environment: the description of everything outside the security perimeter that is relevant to the security of the aircraft or system under consideration. The definition of security environments is equivalent to the <em>external context </em>as used in ISO 27005:2011.</p><p>To learn more about specifying security scope, refer to the following pages:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5c8763ee-b56f-43de-a5a6-d27f6fc99060" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008383 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00045: (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer

- page_id: `304008383`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008383/2026x+Refresh1+Systems+Cybersecurity+Designer
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:17:56.743+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cybersecurity engineering is a critical discipline that ensures safe and secure human experiences with cyber-physical systems. The primary challenge is to preserve trust for connected cyber systems in an evolving digital economy increasingly threatened by unpredictable events.

For the standard [ISO 21434](https://www.iso.org/standard/70918.html), based on a system architecture, the Systems Cybersecurity Designer enables:

- Selection of Assets for protection
- Threat and Cyberattack Modeling
- Risk Evaluation
- Cybersecurity requirements creation to mitigate identified risks

The Systems Cybersecurity Designer supports the following risk assessment methodology:

- Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment as per ISO/SAE 21434:2021

For the standard [DO-326A](https://products.rtca.org/21dja1f/) focusing on an avionics system architecture, the Systems Cybersecurity Designer enables:

- Definition of the Security Scope
- Threat Definition and Attack Path Modeling
- Security Risk Assessment
- Specification of Security Requirements to mitigate identified risks, and evaluation of their effectiveness

The Systems Cybersecurity Designer supports the following risk assessment methodology:

- Cybersecurity Risk Assessment as per RTCA DO-326A/ED-202A

To learn more about Systems Cybersecurity Designer, refer to:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Collaborative modeling' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Generating Cybersecurity Reports' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x Refresh1) Libraries' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304008740 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00047: (2026x Refresh1) TARA

- page_id: `304008740`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/304008740/2026x+Refresh1+TARA
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-29T15:18:05.216+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) Systems Cybersecurity Designer > (2026x Refresh1) ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > (2026x Refresh1) Table
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

The TARA table gathers all elements that have been modeled in the previous steps and gives a global overview of the threat scenario that has to be mitigated, retained, shared, or avoided. The risk value is automatically calculated according to the ISO/SAE 21434:2021 standard.

**Cybersecurity Risk**

An effect of uncertainty on road vehicle cybersecurity expressed in terms of attack feasibility and impact.

**Cybersecurity Control**

A measure that is modifying risk.

**Cybersecurity Claim**

A statement about a risk.

**Cybersecurity Goal**

A concept-level cybersecurity requirement associated with one or more threat scenarios.

##### Creating a TARA Table

If you create a new project using the**ISO 21434 Project**template, then a**TARA**tablealready exists in the**1.4 Risk Treatment and Cybersecurity Control**package.

To create aTARATable

1. In the Containment tree, right-click Risk Treatment and Cybersecurity Control and select Create Diagram. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**and select **TARA** **Table**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword TARA and then select TARA Table. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']** The TARA Table is displayed in the diagram pane of the modeling tool. [ATTACHMENT filename='TARA Table created.png']

##### Adding Threat Scenarios

To add Threat Scenarios to the TARA Table

1. In the TARA Table, click Add Existing. [ATTACHMENT filename='Add Existing.png']
2. From the **Select Threat Scenario******dialog, select the required Threat Scenario. [ATTACHMENT filename='Select TS-Dialog.jpg'] A row is added to the TARA Table, which shows the existing Threat Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Threat Type, Impacted Asset, and Damage Scenarios are automatically added to the TARA Table based on the Damage Scenario Table and Threat Scenarios Table. The association between Threat Scenarios and Damage Scenarios tables is done through failure. The Damage Scenarios which have the same Failure Modes as a given Threat Scenario are taken into account for Risk Values computation.The risk values are automatically computed according to ISO/SAE 21434:2021 standard. Risk values are read-only values.

##### Assigning Risk Treatment Decision

To assign Risk Treatment Decision

- Double-click the cellin the**Risk Treatment Decision** column and the requiredThreat Scenario'srow. From the drop-down list, assign Risk Treatment Decision. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Risk Treatment Decision is assigned in the TARA Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] If the risk treatment decision is Retain, adding a claim is mandatory. In those cases, the cybersecurity goals and controls are notrequired.

##### Adding Cybersecurity Goal

To add a Cybersecurity Goal to the TARA Table

1. Double-click the designated cell in the Cybersecurity Goals column and the required Threat Scenario's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] . [ATTACHMENT filename='Goal creation_Three dots.png']
2. From the **Select Element******dialog, select Cybersecurity Goal. [ATTACHMENT filename='Select Element dialog_Goal creation.png'] The Cybersecurity Goal is added to the TARA Table.
3. [ATTACHMENT filename='Goal creation_Done.png']

- You can also add a safety goal from another project (HARA analysis) as a cybersecurity goal. Doing this will create a clone of the safety goal in your project. To add a safety goal as a cybersecurity goal, follow the same procedure as defined above.
- If the ASIL value of the safety goal is inconsistent with the Safety Risk Value of the threat scenario, a validation rule is triggered and the particular row is displayed in red. You can view the error message in the Active Validation Results pane.
- You can mitigate the error by making the ASIL value and the Safety Risk Value consistent with each other. [ATTACHMENT filename='Error.jpg']

To Generate/Synchronize the Cybersecurity Goals to the TARA Table

- Right-click the threat scenario in the TARA table and select Generate/Synchronize Cybersecurity Goals. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- The cybersecurity goal is autogenerated based on the following formula:
  - [Asset Name]of the[Item]shall be protected against[Threat type]
- If you add an item, asset or a threat type for a threat scenario, the command autogenerates a cybersecurity goal.
- If you update an item, asset or a threat type for a threat scenario, the command synchronizes the autogenerated cybersecurity goal. Following are the two scenarios in which synchronization happens:
  - If you rename an item, asset or a threat type, then the existing autogenerated cybersecurity goal is renamed.
  - If you add/remove an item, asset or a threat type, then an autogenerated cybersecurity goal is added/removed.
- You can also select multiple Threat Scenarios and generate/synchronize cybersecurity goals for all the selected Threat Scenarios at once.

##### Adding Controls

To add Controls to the TARA Table

1. Double-click the designated cell in the Controls column and the required Threat Scenario's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] . [ATTACHMENT filename='Adding control_three dots.png']
2. From the **Select Elements******dialog, select Controls. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Controls are added to the TARA Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Controls are a list of Cybersecurity Requirements. There are 4 types of Cybersecurity Requirements: Functional, Technical, Hardware, and Software.

To ease the process of adding controls, the plugin provides a feature to add the controls with the aid of the Recommend Control command. The controls are recommended on the basis of assigned cybersecurity goals and CWE elements used as attack path steps.

To add controls using the Recommend Control command to the TARA Table

1. Right-click the threat scenario in the TARA table and select Recommended Control, as follows: [ATTACHMENT filename='Recommended Controls.png']
2. From the **Select Elements**dialog, select or remove the recommended controls. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] For requirements to be reflected as recommended controls in the Select Elements dialog, either of these conditions should be satisfied:A Threat scenario should have assigned cybersecurity goals with all the derived requirements. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']A Threat Scenario should have an Attack Path, which itself has a step, which is either a CWE or a Technique. In such case, if the CWE or Technique has a Recommendation from a Cybersecurity Requirement, then that requirement will be automatically proposed by **Recommend control**command. The recommended controls are added to the TARA Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding Claim

To add a Claim to the TARA Table

- Double-click the cell in the **Claims**column and the required Threat Scenario's row and type in the necessary Claim. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] If the risk treatment decision is Retain, adding a claim is mandatory. In those cases, the cybersecurity goals and controls are not required and cannot be specified. Due to some performance reason, the claim does not appear in the containment tree directly after specifying it in the claim's cell. You must save the project to see the claims in the containment tree under the smart package **2.3 Cybersecurity Claims**.

##### Assigning Residual Risk Value

To assign the Residual Risk value in the TARA Table

- Double-click Residual Safety Risk value cell in the Threat Scenario row. From the drop-down list, select the R esidual Safety R isk Value . Follow the same procedure to assign Financial, Operational and Privacy Residual Risk Values. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- You can add custom residual risk value in between the given range of 1.0 to 5.0, such as 1.2, 3.7 or 0.3.
- You can also assign the residual risk value with help of the Specification dialog of the Threat Scenario.
- The maximum value among all the residual risk values viz. Safety, Financial, Operational, and Privacy is considered as the Residual Global Risk Value.

##### TARA Table Example

- The Safety, Financial, Operational, Privacy risk values are calculated automatically by using following formula:
  - Risk Value = 1 + Maximum(Impact) * Aggregated Attack Feasibility Rating [ATTACHMENT filename='Impact Rating.png']
- The maximum value among all the risk values viz. Safety, Financial, Operational, and Privacy is considered as the Global Risk Value.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
