<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574904 space=SCD version=7 -->
## PAGE 00112: Creating a Security Impact Table

- page_id: `249574904`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574904/Creating+a+Security+Impact+Table
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:11:06.770+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > Impact Analysis > Generating Threat Conditions and Security Impacts
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Security Impact Table**

The Security Impact Table is automatically created while [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generating Threat Conditions and Security Impacts' space='']. However, if you still want to create a Security Impact Table, the procedure is outlined below.

##### Creating a Security Impact Table

To create a Security Impact Table

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the L1-Solution package and select Create Diagram.
2. Do one of the following:
  - I n the dialog, expand **DO-326A**and select**L1-Security Impact Table.** [ATTACHMENT filename='Security Impact Table_Select.png']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword security and then select **L1-Security Impact Table.** [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The Security Impact table is created in the Containment tree with the same name as the selected package. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Security Impact table created in the Containment tree with the same name as the selected package.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

However, if you still want to create a Security Impact Table, the procedure is outlined below.</span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3><span data-ref="99c05622-da3c-464d-bc46-27e843e04eb2">Creating a Security Impact Tabl</span>e</h3><p>To create a Security Impact Table</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment tree, right-click the <strong>L1-Solution</strong> package and select <strong>Create Diagram.</strong></li><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">I</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">n the dialog, expand <strong>DO-326A<span> </span></strong>and select<span> <strong>L1-Security Impact Table.</strong></span></span><strong><br /><br /></strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Security Impact Table_Select.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li class="auto-cursor-target">In the search tab, type the keyword security and then select<span> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>L1-Security Impact Table.</strong></span></span><strong><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Security Impact Table_Search.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li></ul></li></ol><p>The Security Impact table is created in the Containment tree with the same name as the selected package.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Security Impact Table_created.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The Security Impact table created in the Containment tree with the same name as the selected package.</span></h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575038 space=SCD version=4 -->
## PAGE 00113: Creating a Sub-Level Package

- page_id: `249575038`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249575038/Creating+a+Sub-Level+Package
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:12:07.256+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Creating a Sub-Level Package

To create a Sub-Level Package

1. In the Containment tree, c reate a new package under the **Security Engineering Process (SecEP)**package and n ame the created package **L4**.
2. In the Specification dialog of the **L4**package:
  1. In the**Applied Stereotype**property cell, click[IMAGE alt='' src=''] , thenselect the **SecurityModelPackage[Package]**stereotype from the list and c lick**Apply.** [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  2. In the Tags property group, double-click the abstractionLevel property, and set its value to 4. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  3. In the Tags property group, double-click the riskEvaluationMethod property , and select the required method from the drop-down list.
    - Select **ISO18045_2022** to have the same behavior as a Level 2 package.
    - Select **Effectiveness** to have the same behavior as a Level 3 package. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  4. Click Close .
3. Create a new package under the L4 package and name it Misuse Cases .
4. Open the Specification dialog of the Misuse Cases package, c lick the**Applied Stereotype**property cell, then click[IMAGE alt='' src=''], select **Misuse CasesPackage[Package]**from the list,****and click**Apply. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
5. Similarly, create other packages such as **Threat Source, Vulnerabilities, Attack Path**, etc., and apply the respective stereotypes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Select <strong>Effectiveness</strong> to have the same behavior as a Level 3 package.</span><br /><br /><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Specification_L4_Method.png" /></ac:image></strong><br /><br /></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="3c50b807-1fb5-4c2e-ad65-60eae3454e2a">Click <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ol></li><li data-uuid="4ba5c908-a7c7-4ed8-a5aa-bdb0de26c211">Create a new package under the <strong>L4 </strong>package and name it <strong>Misuse Cases</strong>.</li><li data-uuid="f783a1c5-9d68-4a6e-905c-6b1453a10c31">Open the Specification dialog of the <strong>Misuse Cases </strong>package, c<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">lick the </span><strong style="text-align: left;">Applied Stereotype</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span> </span>property cell, then click<span> </span></span><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper" style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="11"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image-2025-7-7_17-45-11.png" /></ac:image></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">, select</span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> <strong>Misuse CasesPackage[Package] </strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">from the list,</span><strong> </strong>and click<strong> Apply.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Stereotype_Misuse case.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></span></li><li data-uuid="6df9eee4-66a8-450f-83a6-a8e0124fdd93"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Similarly, create other packages such as <strong>Threat Source, Vulnerabilities, Attack Path</strong>, etc., and apply the respective stereotypes.</span></li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=263094273 space=SCD version=14 -->
## PAGE 00114: Custom Security Evaluation Model

- page_id: `263094273`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/263094273/Custom+Security+Evaluation+Model
- version_number: 14
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:12:09.186+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A **SecurityEvaluationModel** Stereotype has the following tags:

- securityAttributes : list of SecurityAttribute
- impactClassification : EffectSeverity, an Enumeration of EffectSeverityValue
- riskClassification : Risk, an Enumeration of RiskValue
- riskFormula : RiskFormula, an OpaqueBehavior with parameters:
- effectivenessFormula : RiskFormula, an OpaqueBehavior with parameters:

##### Creating a custom SecurityEvaluationModel Stereotype

To create a custom SecurityEvaluationModel Stereotype

1. In the Containment tree, right-click **Model**and select Create Element.
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand General and select Class . [ATTACHMENT filename='Class_Search.png']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword class and then select Class . [ATTACHMENT filename='Class_Type.png']
3. Name the newly created Class element as Custom Evaluation Model in the Containment tree.
4. In the Specification dialog of the****Custom** Evaluation Model**Class**:**
  1. Click the Applied Stereotype property specification cell, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] , select **SecurityEvaluationModel****[Class]** stereotype from the list, and click **Apply**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
  2. Click **Close**.
5. In the Specification dialog of the CustomEvaluationModel Stereotype:
  1. Click the Security Attributes property specification cell, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] , add the required security attributes from the list, and click **OK**.
  2. Click the **Impact Classification** property specification cell and select **DefaultEffectSeverity** from the list.
  3. Click the **Risk Classification** property specification cell and select **DefaultRisk** from the list.
  4. Click the **Effectiveness Formula** property specification cell and select **DefaultEffectivenessFormula** from the list.
  5. Click the **Risk Formula** property specification cell and select **DefaultRiskFormula****** from the list.
  6. Click **Close**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Using the custom SecurityEvaluationModel Stereotype

To use the custom SecurityEvaluationModel Stereotype

1. Open the Specification dialog of the **L1 - Solution** package (package with the **EvaluationModelUsagePakage** stereotype), select **Tags**>**usedModel,**and click **[IMAGE alt='' src=''].** 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. In the Select Security Evaluation Model dialog, select **Custom Evaluation Model**and click **OK**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Confirm the values for the **usedModel**property and click **Close**. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

SecurityAttribute</li><li data-uuid="dd91e0e4-0d86-4fd3-9971-eafecb1dca71"><strong>impactClassification</strong>: EffectSeverity, an Enumeration of EffectSeverityValue</li><li <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574729 space=SCD version=3 -->
## PAGE 00115: Cyber Security Attack Graph

- page_id: `249574729`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574729/Cyber+Security+Attack+Graph
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:07:21.495+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > Table > Threat  scenario
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Creating a Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram

To create aCyber Security Attack Graph Diagram

1. In the Containment tree, right-click any required package and select Create Diagram. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand**ISO 21434**and select **Cyber****Security Attack Graph Diagram**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword graph and then select **Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram** . 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram**** is displayed in the diagram pane of the modeling tool. [ATTACHMENT filename='Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram.png']

##### Adding an Attack Path Step in the Attack Graph

To add a new Attack Path Step in the Attack Graph

- From the diagram palette, select Attack Path Step and c lick on the diagram pane. Name the created Attack Path Step. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To add an existing Attack Path Step, CWE Element, or any Situation in the Attack Graph

- Drag the required Attack Path Step, CWE Element, or any Situation from the Containment tree and drop it in the diagram pane. [ATTACHMENT filename='Attack Steps_Drag and Drop.png']

##### Creating a Causality Relation between Attack Path Steps

To create a Causality Relation between Attack Path Steps

- Do one of the following:
  - Click the attack path step to open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart manipulator toolbar' space='MT'] , select the Causality relationship, and create a relationship. To learn more about creating relationships, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a relationship' space='MT'] [ATTACHMENT filename='Causality Relation_Smart Manipulator.png']
  - From the diagram palette, select the Causality command and then select the required attack path steps . [ATTACHMENT filename='Causality_Diagram Palette.png']

An arrow icon is displayed in the graph which denotes that causality relation is created.[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Generating an Attack Path from the Attack Graph

To generate an Attack Path from the Attack Graph

1. In the attack graph, select the two required attack steps by holding down the Shift key. The selected attack paths are considered as two ends of an attack path.
2. Right-click and select the Generate Attack Paths command. [ATTACHMENT filename='Generate Attack Paths.png']
3. In the Select Attack Paths dialog, select the required attack path from the list of available attack paths and click OK. Loops present in the attack graph are eliminated while calculating the attack path(s). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. In the Select Destination Package dialog, select the package to save the generated attack path(s). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] After generation of an Attack Path, if Attack Graph changes in such a way that the path is not valid anymore, a validation rule is triggered and an error occurs. You can fix the error from the Attack Path, but in such case, the path will not be linked to the graph anymore. Also, no further errors will be reported on that Attack Path if the graph is further modified.The generated attack path(s) are manual attack paths.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="da1d3de1-7fe0-4f45-b5ba-f59f1e700918">Creating a Cyber Security Attack Graph</ac:inline-comment-marker> Diagram</h3><p>To create a<span> Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment tree, right-click<span> </span>any required package<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span>and select<strong><span> </span>Create Diagram.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Create Diagram.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the dialog, expand<span> </span><strong>ISO 21434<span> </span></strong>and select <strong>Cyber</strong><span><strong> Security Attack Graph Diagram</strong></span>.<br /></span><strong><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram_Menu.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li><li class="auto-cursor-target">In the search tab, type the keyword graph and then select<span> <strong>Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram</strong></span><strong>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram_Search.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong>The <span>Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram<strong> </strong></span>is displayed in the diagram pane of the modeling tool.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Cyber Security Attack Graph Diagram.png" /></ac:image></li></ul></li></ol><h3>Adding an Attack Path Step in the Attack Graph</h3><p>To add a new Attack Path Step in the Attack Graph</p><hr /><ul><li>From the diagram palette, select <strong>Attack Path Step</strong> and c<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">lick on the diagram pane. Name the created Attack Path Step.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Attack Path step_Diagram Pallete.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">To add an existing Attack Path Step, CWE Element, or any Situation in the Attack Graph</span></p><hr /><ul><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="975d17d3-ec2c-4d2d-8111-7bee0c82aa15">Drag the required <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Attack Path Step, CWE Element, or any Situation</span></ac:inline-comment-marker> from the Containment tree and drop it in the diagram pane.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Attack Steps_Drag and Drop.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><h3>Creating a Causality Relation between Attack Path Steps</h3><p>To create a Causality Relation between Attack Path Steps</p><hr /><ul><li>Do one of the following:<br /><br /><ul><li>Click the attack path step to open the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Smart manipulator toolbar" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Smart Manipulator toolbar]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, select the Causality relationship, and create a relationship. To learn more about creating relationships, refer to <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MT" ri:content-title="Creating a relationship" /></ac:link><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Causality Relation_Smart Manipulator.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>From the diagram palette, select the Causality command and then select the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="10887742-853d-4e1c-96f3-3f31fdf9f597">required attack path steps</ac:inline-comment-marker>.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Causality_Diagram Palette.png" /></ac:image></li></ul></li></ul><p>An arrow icon is displayed in the graph which denotes that causality relation is created.<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Causality Relation created.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p><h3>Generating an Attack Path from the Attack Graph</h3><p>To generate an Attack Path from the Attack Graph</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the attack graph, select the two required attack steps by holding down the Shift key. The selected attack paths are considered as two ends of an attack path.</p></li><li>Right-click and select the Generate Attack Paths command.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Generate Attack Paths.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Select Attack Paths dialog, select the required attack path from the list of available attack paths and click OK.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d0907153-873c-43f4-9f47-b725a1f54485"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Loops present in the attack graph are eliminated while calculating the attack path(s).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select Attack Path_Dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the Select Destination Package dialog, select the package to save the generated attack path(s).<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select Destination Package_Dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4f1e46a7-c6bb-4d05-a42d-1b4a4967644a"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>After generation of an Attack Path, if Attack Graph changes in such a way that the path is not valid anymore, a validation rule is triggered and an error occurs. You can fix the error from the Attack Path, but in such case, the path will not be linked to the graph anymore. Also, no further errors will be reported on that Attack Path if the graph is further modified.</li><li>The generated attack path(s) are manual attack paths.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574350 space=SCD version=5 -->
## PAGE 00116: Damage scenario

- page_id: `249574350`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574350/Damage+scenario
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2026-06-19T17:50:54.888+02:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > Table
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Damage Scenario**An adverse consequence involving a vehicle or vehicle function and affecting a road user.

**Effect**Allows you to define and manage system and vehicle-level effects that can result in harm.

**Failure**Termination of an intended behavior of an element or an item due to a fault manifestation.

**Operational Situation**A scenario that can occur during a vehicle's life.

**Impact category**Impact rating is done for four categories: Safety, Financial, Operational, and Privacy. The assessment should be done according to the definition given in ISO/SAE 21434:2021(annex F).

- Impact scale By default, the plugin adopts the scale provided in the ISO/SAE 21434:2021 standard: Negligible / Moderate / Major or Severe.

##### Creating a Damage Scenario

To create a Damage Scenario

1. In the Containment tree, right-click **Damage Scenario and Impact Ratings** and select Create Element. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**and select **Damage** **Scenario**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword damage and then select Damage Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Name the created Damage Scenario in the Containment tree and press Enter. The Damage Scenario has the prefix **D****S** which denotes the created element is a Damage Scenario; the number **1** indicates that it is the first Damage Scenario created. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Creating a Damage Scenarios Table

If you create a new project using the**ISO 21434 Project**template, then an**Damage Scenarios Table**already exists in the**1.1 Damage Scenarios and Impact Ratings**package.

To create a Damage ScenarioTable

1. In the Containment tree, right-click Damage Scenario and Impact Ratings and select Create Diagram. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**and select **Damage Scenario Table**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword damage and then select Damage Scenarios Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Damage Scenarios Table is displayed in the diagram pane of the modeling tool. [ATTACHMENT filename='1 ContainmentTreeDamageScenariosTable.png']

##### Adding a Damage Scenario to the Damage Scenarios Table

To add a new Damage Scenario to the Damage Scenarios Table

1. In the Damage Scenario Table, click Add New. A row is added in the Damage Scenarios Table, which shows the new Damage Scenario. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. In the newly created Damage Scenario's row and the **Name**column, double-click the designated cell to name the Damage Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To add an existing Damage Scenario to the Damage Scenarios Table

1. In the Damage Scenarios Table, click Add Existing. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Damage Scenario**dialog, select the required Damage Scenario. A row is added to the Damage Scenarios Table, which shows the existing Damage Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. In the existing Damage Scenario's row and the **Name**column, double-click the designated cell to rename the Damage Scenario. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding a Failure

To add a Failure to the Damage Scenarios Table

1. Double-click the designated cell in the Failure column and the required Damage Scenario's row and click [ATTACHMENT filename='Three Dot _Icon.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='1 ClickThreeDots_Failure.png']
2. From the Select Class dialog, select Failure. [ATTACHMENT filename='2 SelectFailureWindow_v2.png'] The Failure is shown in the Damage Scenarios Table. [ATTACHMENT filename='3 AfterAddingFailure.png']

You can also drag and drop the Failure modes from the Containment tree to the Damage Scenarios Table.

##### Adding an Effect

To add an Effect in the Damage Scenarios Table

1. Double-click the designated cell in the Effect column and the required Damage Scenario's row and click [ATTACHMENT filename='Three Dot _Icon.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='1 ClickThreeDots_Effect.png']
2. From the Select Class dialog, select Effect. [ATTACHMENT filename='2 SelectEffectWindow_v2.png'] The Effect is shown in the Damage Scenarios Table. [ATTACHMENT filename='3 AfterAddingEffect.png']

You can also drag and drop the Effects such as Vehicle Level Effects and System Level Effects from the Containment tree to the Damage Scenarios Table.

##### Adding an Operational Situation

To add an Operational Situation in the Damage Scenarios Table

1. Double-click the designated cell in the Operational Situation column and the required Damage Scenario's row and click [ATTACHMENT filename='Three Dot _Icon.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='1 ClickThreeDots_Operation Situation.png']
2. From the Select Class dialog, select Operational Situation. [ATTACHMENT filename='2 SelectOpSituation_v2.png'] The Operational Situation is added in the Damage Scenarios Table. [ATTACHMENT filename='3 AfterAddingOpSituation.png']

You can also drag and drop the Operation Situations from the Containment tree to the Damage Scenarios Table.

##### Rating SFOP Impact

- The Damage Scenario cannot be rated if there is no Effect associated to it.
- All the Damage Scenarios sharing the same Effect get the same impact ratings. In such case, if you update any impact rating(s) for one Damage Scenario, the impact rating(s) for other Damage Scenarios are updated.

To rate the SFOP Impact

- Double-click each cell, namely**Safety Impact**, **Financial Impact**, **Operational Impact**, and **Privacy Impact**in the Damage Scenario row. From the drop-down list, select the impact rating. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Impact rating is done in the Damage Scenarios Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The plugin behavior when you use the Effect element from the used project in your local project:

- When you update the SFOP Impact ratings of the damage scenario, a cloned Effect element is created in your local project and is visible in the Containment tree. The following notification message is displayed with the cloned Effect element link at the bottom right corner of the modeling tool. [ATTACHMENT filename='Cloned_Notification.png']

The plugin behavior when you use the Effect element from the used project in the TWC server project:

- When you update the SFOP Impact ratings of the damage scenario, a cloned Effect element is created in your server project and is visible in the Containment tree. The following notification message is displayed with the cloned Effect element link at the bottom right corner of the modeling tool. [ATTACHMENT filename='Cloned_Notification.png']

The plugin behavior when you use the Effect element from your own TWC server project:

- When you update the SFOP ratings of the damage scenario and the Effect element is in a read-only state, the Effect element gets locked automatically by you.
- When you update the SFOP ratings of the damage scenario and the Effect element is locked by another user, the following notification is displayed with the locked Effect element link at the bottom right corner of the modeling tool. [ATTACHMENT filename='Locked_Notification.PNG']

##### Damage Scenarios Table Example 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### INFO: References

References

- ISO 26262-1:2018 Road vehicles-Functional safety
- Tables and diagrams

that can result in harm.</span></p><p><strong>Failure<br /></strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Termination of an intended behavior of an element or an item due to a fault <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574851 space=SCD version=5 -->
## PAGE 00117: DO-3262A Concept

- page_id: `249574851`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574851/DO-3262A+Concept
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-09-19T11:53:46.085+02:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### DO-326A Process

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Index of DO-326A

The cybersecurity risk assessment process described in [DO-326A](https://products.rtca.org/21dja1f/) is a methodology used in the Aerospace and defense industry to identify, evaluate, and mitigate cyber threats affecting avionics systems. It emphasizes a multi-level approach, starting at the aircraft level and extending to individual systems, ensuring that cybersecurity is integrated throughout the system.

The following is the standard procedure followed in DO-326A:

1. Define the Security Scope by considering the system of interest and its interfaces to be protected. Identify Security Assets relevant to aircraft functions along with their security attributes. Define Indirect Assets (e.g., Crew Member, Passengers).
2. Identify the Threat Conditions by determining potential attackers, their capabilities, and possible threat scenarios for each Security Asset and their Impact on Indirect Assets.
3. Analyze the Attack Paths by mapping how an attacker could exploit vulnerabilities across multiple layers.
4. Assess the Risk by evaluating the consequences and acceptability of threats at the multi-system level in a Pre-Threat Scenario Table.
5. Define the Security Requirements by specifying measures to prevent or mitigate threats.
6. Implement the Security Measures by applying technical or procedural protections in the refined functional architecture and in a Post-Threat Scenario Table.
7. Evaluate the Effectiveness by assessing how well the measures protect Level 3 systems or below against identified threats.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3>DO-326A Process<br /><br /></h3><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:border="true" ac:height="837" ac:width="1455"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Concept page_Index of DO326A.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Index of DO-326A</h6><p>The cybersecurity risk assessment process described in <a href="https://products.rtca.org/21dja1f/">DO-326A</a> is a methodology used in the Aerospace and defense industry to identify, evaluate, and mitigate cyber threats affecting avionics systems. It emphasizes a multi-level approach, starting at the aircraft level and extending to individual systems, ensuring that cybersecurity is integrated throughout the system.</p><p>The following is the standard procedure followed in DO-326A:</p><ol><li data-uuid="3f297210-7600-4acb-a7b0-13978988223f"><strong>Define the Security Scope</strong> by considering the system of interest and its interfaces to be protected. Identify Security Assets relevant to aircraft functions along with their security attributes. Define Indirect Assets (e.g., Crew Member, Passengers).</li><li data-uuid="b8936983-c930-4b3e-acc5-50e9b6fe957b"><strong>Identify the Threat Conditions</strong> by determining potential attackers, their capabilities, and possible threat scenarios for each Security Asset and their Impact on Indirect Assets.</li><li data-uuid="ffb35d10-ed9e-4551-87c5-066a209d6d0f"><strong>Analyze the Attack Paths</strong> by mapping how an attacker could exploit vulnerabilities across multiple layers.</li><li data-uuid="4a6c04b3-b785-4f58-8b08-a965ef416fdb"><strong>Assess the Risk</strong> by evaluating the consequences and acceptability of threats at the multi-system level in a Pre-Threat Scenario Table.</li><li data-uuid="76dd1cac-9bc7-4da8-a2f7-0a81d921edf9"><strong>Define the Security Requirements</strong> by specifying measures to prevent or mitigate threats.</li><li data-uuid="097a2d72-6eb5-4da8-af50-ccf246567096"><strong>Implement the Security Measures</strong> by applying technical or procedural protections in the refined functional architecture and in a Post-Threat Scenario Table.</li><li data-uuid="d8c1a76b-49ac-4d65-8560-8b99d3e36fff"><strong>Evaluate the Effectiveness</strong> by assessing how well the measures protect Level 3 systems or below against identified threats.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574847 space=SCD version=6 -->
## PAGE 00118: DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment

- page_id: `249574847`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574847/DO-326A+Preliminary+Security+Risk+Assessment
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:09:50.764+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cybersecurity engineering is a crucial discipline to ensure safe and secure interactions with onboard systems in the increasingly connected and automated Aerospace & Defense Industry. The main challenge lies in maintaining trust in connected systems in a rapidly evolving threat landscape that is growing in complexity.

System Cybersecurity Designer enables engineers to define the Security Scope, potential threats, and attacks, while defining mitigation strategies to ensure system resilience throughout an aircraft lifecycle. The tool is designed to support the RTCA DO-326A/ED-202A standard, offering a structured approach for continuous security risk assessment from system design.

The [DO-326A](https://products.rtca.org/21dja1f/) standard, titled *Airworthiness Security Process Specification*, provides guidance for the implementation of an airworthiness cybersecurity process. It applies to aircraft systems that include embedded software and hardware, ensuring that cybersecurity risks are identified, analyzed, and mitigated throughout the system lifecycle. DO-326A is complemented by related documents like DO-355A *(Information Security Guidance for Continuing Airworthiness*) and DO-356A (*Airworthiness Security Methods and Considerations*), forming a framework for managing cyber risks in avionics.

Rather than prescribing specific technologies, DO-326A describes a process-oriented approach to cybersecurity that integrates with safety processes (e.g., ARP4761) and supports certification efforts. It includes methods such as vulnerability identification (Threat Scenarios, Attack Paths), security risk assessment, and threat modeling, ensuring compliance with airworthiness standards. Detecting vulnerabilities at the earliest development stage minimizes the impact on cost and development time.

The Cybersecurity Designer DO-326A enables cybersecurity engineers to:

- Ensure lifecycle-wide security, integrating cybersecurity from the early stages through maintenance by securing avionics architectures and their interfaces between sub-systems. To achieve this, Cybersecurity engineers define assets, threat scenarios, vulnerabilities, and mitigation strategies.

The Cybersecurity Designer role supports the following core aspects of the DO-326A standard:

- Security Scope Definition : To begin, Cybersecurity Engineers identify the perimeter of evaluation, its boundaries, and potential vulnerabilities that could compromise the safety of operations due to an attack.
- Security Risk Assessment : This activity evaluates the likelihood and impact of threat scenarios, enabling the identification of Security Requirements. It is coupled with system safety assessments to ensure coherence between safety and security domains.
- Security Development Effectiveness (SDE) : Based on the severity of the above Security Risk Assessment and its potential threats to airworthiness, the SDE will guide the rigor of the security design and verification activities.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To install and use the Systems Cybersecurity Designer, ensure that one of the following modeling tools is installed:

- Magic Cyber Systems Engineer
- Magic Cyber Systems of Systems Architect
- Cameo Systems Modeler - Architect Edition
- Cameo Systems Modeler - Enterprise Edition
- Cameo Enterprise Architecture
- Magic Draw (any version) with SysML plugin installed

To learn more about how to download the installation files, refer to [Downloading installation files](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=downloading-installation-files&version=latest).

To install and use the Systems Cybersecurity Designer, ensure that the following plugins are installed in your modeling tool:

- Cameo Safety and Reliability Analyzer
- ISO 26262 plugin

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Cybersecurity engineering is a crucial discipline to ensure safe and secure interactions with onboard systems in the increasingly connected and automated Aerospace &amp; Defense Industry. The main challenge lies in maintaining trust in connected systems in a rapidly evolving threat landscape that is growing in complexity.</p><p>System Cybersecurity Designer enables engineers to define the Security Scope, potential threats, and attacks, while defining mitigation strategies to ensure system resilience throughout an aircraft lifecycle. The tool is designed to support the RTCA DO-326A/ED-202A standard, offering a structured approach for continuous security risk assessment from system design.</p><p>The <a href="https://products.rtca.org/21dja1f/">DO-326A</a> standard, titled <em>Airworthiness Security Process Specification</em>, provides guidance for the implementation of an airworthiness cybersecurity process. It applies to aircraft systems that include embedded software and hardware, ensuring that cybersecurity risks are identified, analyzed, and mitigated throughout the system lifecycle. DO-326A is complemented by related documents like DO-355A <em>(Information Security Guidance for Continuing Airworthiness</em>) and DO-356A (<em>Airworthiness Security Methods and Considerations</em>), forming a framework for managing cyber risks in avionics.</p><p>Rather than prescribing specific technologies, DO-326A describes a process-oriented approach to cybersecurity that integrates with safety processes (e.g., ARP4761) and supports certification efforts. It includes methods such as vulnerability identification (Threat Scenarios, Attack Paths), security risk assessment, and threat modeling, ensuring compliance with airworthiness standards. Detecting vulnerabilities at the earliest development stage minimizes the impact on cost and development time.</p><p>The Cybersecurity Designer DO-326A enables cybersecurity engineers to:</p><ul><li data-uuid="e5ee2d84-a9c8-4ffa-8f21-1c5e0b6a9522">Ensure lifecycle-wide security, integrating cybersecurity from the early stages through maintenance by securing avionics architectures and their interfaces between sub-systems. To achieve this, Cybersecurity engineers define assets, threat scenarios, vulnerabilities, and mitigation strategies.</li></ul><p>The Cybersecurity Designer role supports the following core aspects of the DO-326A standard:</p><ul><li data-uuid="9900e6a0-c5b8-48e9-8f15-67c2b1263c05"><strong>Security Scope Definition</strong>: To begin, Cybersecurity Engineers identify the perimeter of evaluation, its boundaries, and potential vulnerabilities that could compromise the safety of operations due to an attack.</li><li data-uuid="57c4b80c-bef0-45f1-925f-cef4a6757d46"><strong>Security Risk Assessment</strong>: This activity evaluates the likelihood and impact of threat scenarios, enabling the identification of Security Requirements. It is coupled with system safety assessments to ensure coherence between safety and security domains.</li><li data-uuid="439e3c04-cc33-4628-9194-90cee862d506"><strong>Security Development Effectiveness (SDE)</strong>: Based on the severity of the above Security Risk Assessment and its potential threats to airworthiness, the SDE will guide the rigor of the security design and verification activities.</li></ul><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6f459f96-4d19-47a8-8187-d08bea2c157e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To install and use the Systems Cybersecurity Designer, ensure that one of the following modeling tools is installed:</span></p><ul><li><p>Magic Cyber Systems Engineer</p></li><li><p>Magic Cyber Systems of Systems Architect</p></li><li><p>Cameo Systems Modeler - Architect Edition</p></li><li><p>Cameo Systems Modeler - Enterprise Edition</p></li><li><p>Cameo Enterprise Architecture</p></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Magic Draw (any version) with SysML plugin installed</span></li></ul><p>To learn more about how to download the installation files, refer to <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=downloading-installation-files&amp;version=latest">Downloading installation files</a>.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To install and use the Systems Cybersecurity Designer, ensure that the following plugins are installed in your modeling tool:</span></p><ul><li>Cameo Safety and Reliability Analyzer</li><li>ISO 26262 plugin</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574854 space=SCD version=7 -->
## PAGE 00119: DO-326A Project

- page_id: `249574854`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574854/DO-326A+Project
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:10:06.083+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Creating DO-326A Project

To create a new DO-326A Project

1. Do one of the following:
  - From the File menu, select New Project from the drop-down list. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - Click Create New Project on the welcome screen. [ATTACHMENT filename='Create New Project_Command.png']
  - Press Ctrl + Shift + N.
2. In the New Project dialog, select DO-326A Project. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Specify the file name in the Name box.
4. Click the[IMAGE alt='' src='']button to select the location to store the project in a folder.
5. Select the checkbox to automatically create a folder for the project in the specified location.
6. Click **OK**.

A new DO-326A Project is displayed with the default packages in the Containment tree and the Index.

If any dependent plugin(s) is not installed, a notification message will be displayed listing the required plugin(s) while launching the modeling tool.

##### DO-326A Project Template

A default DO-326A project template is displayed once you open the project. This template is provided to help you initiate the process.

**Containment Tree**

The Containment tree displays the default packages. A separate package is created for each System Engineering Level in the Containment tree, which also includes the underlying packages and elements. You can add, modify, or delete any element or package from the Containment tree.

**Index**

The Index displays the default information related to the DO-326A project. You can navigate to the tables and diagram by clicking the icons. Only the default tables and elements are displayed in the Index. The Index will not display any table or element created later.

If you press the CTRL key and double-click any table's icon, the table opens in a new tab.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### DO-326A Index

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574453 space=SCD version=2 -->
## PAGE 00120: Effect

- page_id: `249574453`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574453/Effect
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:08:17.738+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > Table > Damage scenario
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Creating an Effect

To create an Effect

1. In the Containment tree, right-click**Damage Scenario****and Impact Ratings**and select Create Element. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**and select **Vehicle Level Effect**or **System Level Effect**. **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
  - In the search tab, type the keyword effect and then select Vehicle Level Effect or System Level Effect. **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**
3. Name the created Effect in the Containment tree and press Enter. 
 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

##### Adding Effects to the Effects Table

Using the Effects Table you can only add nested Effects.

To add an Effects

1. In the Effects Table, select an Effect and then click Add Nested. A row is added in the Effects Table, which shows the nested Effect. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. In the newly created Effect's row and the **Name**column, double-click the designated cell to name the Effect. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding Relevant Element in the Effects Table

To add Relevant To

1. Double-click the designated cell in the**Relevant Element** column and the required Effect's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the **Select Elements******dialog, select Relevant Element. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Relevant Element is added to the Effects Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575034 space=SCD version=5 -->
## PAGE 00121: Effectiveness Method

- page_id: `249575034`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249575034/Effectiveness+Method
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:12:01.548+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > Post Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

### Opening the Effectiveness Method dialog

To open the Effectiveness Method dialog

1. After implementing security measures in the Post Threat Scenario Table, right-click the affiliated Lower Threat Scenario and select Security> Open Effectiveness dialog.... 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Effectiveness Method dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='Effectiveness dialog.png']
2. Classify the security measures and rate their effectiveness to lower the security risk, from *Negotiable* (Yellow) to *Acceptable* (Green). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h1>Opening the Effectiveness Method dialog</h1><p>To open the Effectiveness Method dialog</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="9d4e6cf6-d764-4027-8615-81987d3507f8">After implementing security measures in the Post Threat Scenario Table, right-click the affiliated Lower Threat Scenario and select <strong>Security&gt; Open Effectiveness dialog....<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Effectiveness dialog command.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong>The Effectiveness Method dialog opens.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:width="1300"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Effectiveness dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><strong><br /></strong></li><li data-uuid="49b8a78d-d2d1-4bc2-bc08-eac944bcf1e5"><p>Classify the security measures and rate their effectiveness to lower the security risk, from <em>Negotiable</em> (Yellow) to <em>Acceptable</em> (Green).<br /><br /><ac:image ac:width="1300"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Effectiveness dialog_Done.png" /></ac:image></p></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574913 space=SCD version=4 -->
## PAGE 00122: Extracting a Loss of Security Attribute

- page_id: `249574913`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574913/Extracting+a+Loss+of+Security+Attribute
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:11:16.233+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > Impact Analysis
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Extracting Loss of Security Attribute from a Threat Condition

To extract a Loss of Security Attribute from a Threat Condition

- In the Containment tree, right-click the L1 AcFct 1 provide seating accommodations Availability Impact threat condition, and select Security>Extract Loss of Security Attribute command. [ATTACHMENT filename='Loss of Security Attribute_Command.png']

The Loss of L1 AcFct 1 provide seating accommodations Availability Impact element is created in the Containment tree.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Loss of L1 AcFct 1 provide seating accommodations Availability Impact element created in the Containment tree.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="69d68a6a-0a04-4bca-8b7f-d10251c5a2ba">Extracting Loss of Security Attribute from a Threat Con</ac:inline-comment-marker>dition</h3><p>To extract a Loss of Security Attribute from a Threat Condition</p><hr /><ul><li>In the Containment tree, right-click the <strong>L1 AcFct 1 provide seating accommodations Availability Impact</strong> threat condition, and select <strong>Security&gt;Extract Loss of Security Attribute</strong> command.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Loss of Security Attribute_Command.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><p>The Loss of L1 AcFct 1 provide seating accommodations Availability Impact element is created in the Containment tree.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Loss of Security Attribute_created.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Loss of L1 AcFct 1 provide seating accommodations Availability Impact element created in the Containment tree.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574427 space=SCD version=2 -->
## PAGE 00123: Failure Mode

- page_id: `249574427`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574427/Failure+Mode
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:08:25.254+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > Table > Damage scenario
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Creating a Failure Mode

To create a Failure Mode

1. In the Containment tree, right-click**Damage Scenario****and Impact Ratings**and select Create Element. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**and select **Failure Mode**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword failure and then select Failure Mode. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Name the created Failure Mode in the Containment tree and press Enter. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding Failure Mode to the Failure Modes Table

To add a new Failure Mode to the Failure Modes Table

1. In the Failure Modes Table, click Add New. A row is added in the Failure Modes Table, which shows the new Failure Mode. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select between the FailureMode and MalfucntioningBehavior . [ATTACHMENT filename='Select the failure mode or Malfunction Behavior.png']
3. In the newly created Failure Mode's row and the**Name**column, double-click the designated cell to name the Failure Mode. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To add an existing Failure Mode to the Failure Modes Table

1. In the Failure Modes Table, click Add Existing. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. From the**Select Failure Mode**dialog, select the required Failure Mode. A row is added to the Failure Modes Table, which shows the existing Failure Mode. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. In the existing Failure Mode's row and the**Name**column, double-click the designated cell to rename the Failure Mode. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Adding Relevant To in the Failure Modes Table

To add Relevant To

1. Double-click the designated cell in the **Relevant To** column and the required Failure Mode's row and click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. [ATTACHMENT filename='Relevant To_Three Dot.png']
2. Fromthe**Select Elements******dialog, select Relevant To. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Relevant To is added to the Failure Modes Table. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

/><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Add Existing_Failure Mode.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></strong></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">From the<span> </span><strong>Select Failure Mode </strong>dialog, select the required Failure Mode. A row is added to the Failure Modes Table, which shows the existing Failure Mode.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select Failure Mode dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the existing Failure Mode's row and the<span> </span><strong>Name<span> </span></strong>column, double-click the designated cell to rename the Failure Mode.<br /><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Failure Mode added.png" /></ac:image></span><br /></span></li></ol><h3>Adding Relevant To in the Failure Modes Table</h3><p>To add Relevant To</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Double-click the designated cell in the <strong>Relevant To</strong> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">column and the required Failure Mode's row and cl<span>ick <span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size" style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper confluence-embedded-manual-size"><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="15"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Three Dot _Icon.png" /></ac:image></span></span>.</span></span><br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Relevant To_Three Dot.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">From<span> </span>the<span> </span><strong>Select Elements</strong><strong><span> </span></strong>dialog, select Relevant To.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Select Elements dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br />The Relevant To is added to the Failure Modes Table.<br /><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Relevant To added.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574820 space=SCD version=9 -->
## PAGE 00124: Functional Cybersecurity Concept

- page_id: `249574820`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574820/Functional+Cybersecurity+Concept
- version_number: 9
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:07:32.023+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity > Table
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Cybersecurity Requirement Diagram displays cybersecurity goals, cybersecurity requirements, and their relations. The main purpose of this diagram is to create requirements that cover the goals defined in TARA.

##### Deriving Cybersecurity Requirements

Cybersecurity Requirements are derived from Cybersecurity Goals defined in a TARA table. You can use the Cybersecurity Requirement Diagram to derive four types of Cybersecurity Requirements: Functional, Technical, Software, and Hardware.

To derive a Cybersecurity Requirement

1. From the Index page, open the Functional Cybersecurity Concept.
2. Find the Cybersecurity Goal you want to derive the C ybersecurity Requirement from and drag it to the diagram.
3. Create the Cybersecurity Requirement you want to derive by clicking it in the diagram palette and clicking an empty space on the diagram pane.
4. Name the created C ybersecurity Requirement and write the required text.
5. Create a derived relationship from the C ybersecurity Requirement to the Safety Goal as displayed below. [ATTACHMENT filename='Cybersecurity GoalsAndRequirements.png']

##### Creating a Cybersecurity Goal

To create a Cybersecurity Goal

1. In the Containment tree, right-click Functional Cybersecurity Concept and select Create Element. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO** **21434** and select **Cybersecurity Goal**. [ATTACHMENT filename='Cybersecurity Goal Menu.png']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword goals and then select **Cybersecurity Goal** . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Name the created Cybersecurity Goal in the Containment tree. The cybersecurity goal has the prefix CG, which denotes that the created element is c ybersecurity goals; the number 1 indicates that it is the first c ybersecurity goal**** created. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can also create a Cybersecurity Goal in the Cybersecurity Goal Table or by using the diagram panel.

##### Creating a Cybersecurity Requirement

To create a Cybersecurity Requirement

1. In the Containment tree, right-click Functional Cybersecurity Concept and select Create Element. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, expand **ISO 21434**. From the drop-down list, choose one of the following:
    - Functional Cybersecurity Requirement
    - Hardware Cybersecurity Requirement
    - Software Cybersecurity Requirement
    - Technical Cybersecurity Requirement [ATTACHMENT filename='Cybersecurity Requirement_Menu.jpg']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword goals and then select the required Cybersecurity Requirement . [ATTACHMENT filename='Cybersecurity Requirement_Search.jpg']
3. Name the created Cybersecurity Requirement in the Containment tree. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can also create a cybersecurity requirement in the cybersecurity requirement tables.

##### Cybersecurity Assurance Level

TheCybersecurity Assurance Level (CAL) classification scheme is used to specify a set of assurance requirements for components/items in terms of levels of rigor. These levels of rigor provide confidence that assets of an item are developed with adequate protection. A CAL value is specified on a Cybersecurity Goal and is inherited by all derived requirements. This value specifies the amount of rigor required to address relevant threat scenarios during product development. By default, there are four CAL levels CAL1 to CAL4 representing Low to High Cybersecurity assurance.

To assign a Cybersecurity Assurance Level

1. In the Containment tree, double- click the Cybersecurity Assurance dependency matrix. [ATTACHMENT filename='CAL_Containment tree.png']
2. In the Cybersecurity Assurance dependency matrix, double click the designated cell in the desired cybersecurity goal's row and the CAL value's column. You can modify the dependency matrix based on your requirements. To learn more about modifying dependency matrix, refer to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Dependency Matrix' space='MT']. [ATTACHMENT filename='CAL matrix.png']

You can also create custom CAL values and assign the custom created CAL value to any cybersecurity goal.

To create custom CAL value and assign it to the cybersecurity goal

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the desired package , select Create Element and select Class.
2. Assign CybersecurityAssuraneLevel [Class] stereotype to the created element. [ATTACHMENT filename='Assiging stereotype.png']
3. Assign AbstractCybersecurityAssuranceLevel as Base Classifier to the created element. [ATTACHMENT filename='Applying Base Classifier.png']
4. Drag and drop the package containing the custom CAL value onto the column scope. [ATTACHMENT filename='Column scope change.png']
5. Double-click the designated cell in the cybersecurity goal's row and custom CAL value's column.

##### Functional Cybersecurity Concept Table

The Functional Cybersecurity Concept Table provides an overview of goals & requirements to be implemented to secure assets.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249575050 space=SCD version=2 -->
## PAGE 00125: Generating  Cybersecurity Reports

- page_id: `249575050`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249575050/Generating+Cybersecurity+Reports
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T11:01:34.096+02:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard' space='MT']can generate reports that include data on TARA(Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment).The TARA report presents a comprehensive overview of identified potential threats and their associated risks. It categorizes these threats and provides an analysis of the risks involved.

To generate a report

1. In the main menu of your modeling tool, select**Tools**>**Report Wizard**.
2. Select the TARA Report Template to generate a report containing TARA data and click Next . [ATTACHMENT filename='TARA_Report.png']
3. To include built-in data in the report, select Built-in and click Next.
4. To generate the TARA report, select the packages containing TARA elements and then click Next . 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] To learn more about selecting report data, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Select Element Scope pane' space='MT'].
5. Configure the report file by specifying the report file location and image format. Then Click **Generate**. To learn more about configuring output options, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generate Output pane' space='MT'].

Following is the example of the **TARA**report.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of the **TARA**report.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574891 space=SCD version=6 -->
## PAGE 00126: Generating Threat Conditions and Security Impacts

- page_id: `249574891`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574891/Generating+Threat+Conditions+and+Security+Impacts
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:10:57.742+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > Impact Analysis
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Security Attributes**

Security attributes are fundamental properties of the information and systems that must be protected to ensure cybersecurity.

- Availability : Ensuring authorized users have access to information and associated assets when required.
- Integrity : Safeguarding the accuracy and completeness of information and processing methods.
- Confidentiality : Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to have access.

**Threat Condition**

A condition affecting the airplane and/or its occupants, either direct or consequential, which is caused by or contributed to, one or more acts of intentional, unauthorized, and electronic interaction.

##### Generating Threat Conditions and Security Impacts

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

Security assets must be identified before generating the Threat Conditions and Security Impacts. To learn more, refer to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Identifying Security Assets' space='']page.

To generate Threat Conditions and Security Impacts

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the L1 AcFct 1 to provide seating accommodations security asset and select the **Security>** Generate Threat Conditions & Security Impacts command. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
The Generate Threat Conditions & Security Impacts dialog opens. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. In the Generate Threat Conditions & Security Impacts dialog, click the Create Default button to automatically create a package with the same name as the selected Security Asset. You can also save the generated Threat Conditions and Security Impacts in the custom-created package.To save the generated Threat Conditions and Security Impacts in the custom-created packageCreate a custom package under the **L1 – Solution**package.In the Generate Threat Conditions & Security Impacts dialog, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to open the Select Destination Package dialog.In the Select Destination Package dialog, select the custom-created package and click **OK**.
3. By default, all the Indirect Assets and Flight Phases present in the Indirect Assets and Flight Phases packages are added in the Generate Threat Conditions & Security Impacts dialog. To remove any Indirect Assets or a Flight Phase, select the required Indirect Asset or Flight Phase and click [ATTACHMENT filename='Remove Icon.png'] . In case you remove any Indirect Asset and Flight Phase by mistake, follow the procedure outlined below to add them back.To add any Indirect Assets and Flight PhasesClick the[IMAGE alt='' src='']icon beside the Indirect Assets or Flight Phase section to open the Select Indirect Assets or Select Flight Phases dialog.In the Select Indirect Assets or Select Flight Phases dialog, add the required Indirect Assets or Flight Phases and click**OK**
4. In the Generation Pattern drop-down list, select the One Threat Condition per Security Attribute or One Threat Condition for all Security Attribute option. By default, **One Threat Condition per Security Attribute**generation pattern is selected in the dialog. [ATTACHMENT filename='Pattern_selection.png']
5. Click OK .

The Security Impact table, along with the Threat Condition, is created under the default destination packagein the Containment tree. The Threat Condition has the prefix**TC**, which denotes that the created element is a Threat Condition; the number**1******indicates count.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Security Impact table, along with the Threat Conditions created under the default destination package in the Containment tree.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Threat Condition and Security Impact Table present in the diagram pane of the modeling tool.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

mistake, follow the procedure outlined below to add them back.</p><p><span>To add any Indirect Assets and Flight Phases</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span>C</span><span>lick the </span><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="13"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Edit Icon.png" /></ac:image><span> icon beside the Indirect Assets or Flight Phase section to open the Select Indirect Assets or Select Flight Phases dialog. </span></li><li><span>In the  Select Indirect Assets or Select Flight Phases dialog, add the required Indirect Assets or Flight Phases and click </span><strong>OK</strong></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>In the Generation Pattern drop-down list, select the <strong>One Threat Condition per Security Attribute</strong> or <strong>One Threat Condition for all Security Attribute </strong>option.<br /> <br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="db985db1-7bbc-478e-b9fb-659494659f03"><ac:rich-text-body><p>By default, <strong>One Threat Condition per Security Attribute </strong>generation pattern is selected in the dialog.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Pattern_selection.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p>The Security Impact table, along with the Threat Condition, is created under the default destination package<span> in the Containment tree. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The Threat Condition has the prefix </span><strong style="text-align: left;">TC</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">, which denotes that the created element is a Threat Condition; the number<span> </span></span><strong style="text-align: left;">1</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><strong> </strong>indicates count.</span><br /></span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="SI Table and TC.png" /></ac:image></span></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>The Security Impact table, along with the Threat Conditions created under the default destination package <span>in the Containment tree.<br /></span></span></h6><p style="text-align: center;"><span><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Generate TC and SI_Table.png" /></ac:image></span></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span><span>Threat Condition and Security Impact Table present in the diagram pane of the modeling tool.</span></span></h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574875 space=SCD version=11 -->
## PAGE 00127: Identifying Security Assets

- page_id: `249574875`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574875/Identifying+Security+Assets
- version_number: 11
- version_date: `2026-04-20T09:00:59.238+02:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment > Impact Analysis > Security Scope
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Basic Concepts

**Security Assets**

The logical and physical resources of the aircraft, which contribute to the airworthiness of the aircraft, including functions, systems, items, data, interfaces, processes and information.

##### Identifying L1 level function as Security Assets

To identify an L1 level function as a security asset in the System Engineering Project(SEP)

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an L1 level function and click Stereotype . [ATTACHMENT filename='Select Stereotype.png'] e
2. Do one of the following:
  - In the dialog, select**SecurityAsset[Class]**and click **Apply.** [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the search tab, type the keyword asset, select SecurityAsset[Class], and click **Apply**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The L1 level function is defined as a Security Asset in the Containment tree. A small icon identifies the element as a Security Asset. You can choose whether the icon sould be diplayed on the element through the **Environment**options (**Options**> **Environment**> **Cybesecurity**> **Show Icons in Containment Tree**).

You can also assign the Stereotype to any L1 level function by using the **Specification**dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### L1 level function defined as a Security Asset in the Containment tree.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574873 space=SCD version=6 -->
## PAGE 00128: Impact Analysis

- page_id: `249574873`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574873/Impact+Analysis
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-11-18T11:10:42.317+01:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > DO-326A Preliminary Security Risk Assessment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In the context of DO-326A, **Aircraft Level/Impact Analysis** is the first step in the cybersecurity assurance process. It is concerned with evaluating the potential impact of a cybersecurity threat on aircraft safety, operations, and regulatory compliance. This level considers the aircraft as a complete, integrated system, including all its interconnected electronic systems, networks, software, and interfaces (both internal and external). It is not yet about individual components, but how they collectively contribute to the aircraft's airworthiness.

To learn more, refer to the following pages:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

It is concerned with evaluating the potential impact of a cybersecurity threat on aircraft safety, operations, and regulatory compliance. This level considers the aircraft as a complete, integrated system, including all its interconnected electronic systems, networks, software, and interfaces (both internal and external). It is not yet about individual components, but how they collectively contribute to the aircraft's airworthiness.</p><p>To learn more<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">, refer to the following pages:</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="05c17c50-5fa3-46aa-8926-5c9d937b99ec" /></span></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574181 space=SCD version=1 -->
## PAGE 00130: ISO 21434 Concept

- page_id: `249574181`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574181/ISO+21434+Concept
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-04-17T11:02:36.090+02:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer > ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### TARA process

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### TARA process

The TARA (Threat Analysisand Risk Assessment) process is described in Chapter 15 of ISO/SAE 21434:2021. It is a methodology used to identify and assess cyber security threats and vulnerabilities beginning with the design phase of a product.

The following is the standard procedure followed in TARA:

1. Define the items you want to study. These items are components or sets of components of the considered system.
2. For each item identify t he assets to be protected and allocate CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability) properties for each asset.
3. Define any damage scenarios that can affect a vehicle system/function or a road user. After identifying those damage scenarios, you need to rate their impact in terms of Safety, Financial, Operational, and Privacy.
4. Create threat scenarios that can lead to the identified damage scenarios and rate them in terms of feasibility. Threat scenarios that lead to an asset compromise can be described by one or several attack paths. Each attack path is rated with an attack feasibility value.
5. Assess the risks. The risk is the probability that the threat will occur and entails the damage scenario impact. The risk valu e is automaticallycomputed based on this formula from ISO/SAE 21434:2021, Annex H: Risk = 1 + Impact x Feasibility.
6. According to the computed risk value, decide the risk treatment for each threat scenario: Retain, Reduce, Share, or Avoid. Depending on the risk treatment decision, determine cybersecurity claims or goals that will eventually be detailed in the cybersecurity requirements (functional, technical, hardware, or software type).

An Item is a part of the system architecture to be protected. An Item with a Functional Cybersecurity Concept (output of the study) is the system architecture with additional requirements and claims that ensures a secure system.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

It is a methodology used to identify and assess cyber security threats and vulnerabilities beginning with the design phase of a product.</p><p>The following is the standard procedure followed in TARA:</p><ol style="text-align: left;"><li>Define the items you want to study. These items are components or sets of components of the considered system.</li><li>For each item identify t<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="af0ba61b-9718-49e9-a668-3aa8aeead8c3"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c05e0205-3381-4c26-8d3b-1c549d91a8fd">he assets</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker> to be protected and allocate CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability) properties for each asset.</li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="3052ec32-3de2-449c-bfd4-0a2305aae43e"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2c6a74a6-ec8c-4b17-9c7f-c36e00ca043c">Define any dama</ac:inline-comment-marker>ge scenarios</ac:inline-comment-marker> that can affect a vehicle system/function or a road user. After identifying those damage scenarios, you need to rate their impact in terms of Safety, Financial, Operational, and Privacy.</li><li>Create threat scenarios that can lead to the identified damage scenarios and rate them in terms of feasibility. Threat scenarios that lead to an asset compromise can be described by one or several attack paths. Each attack path is rated with an attack feasibility value.</li><li>Assess the risks. The risk is the probability that the threat will occur and entails the damage scenario impact. The risk valu<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="17f8e5a8-8899-4fa5-a8e1-c7971384ff30">e is <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">automatically </span>computed</ac:inline-comment-marker> based on this formula from ISO/SAE 21434:2021,<span> </span><em>Annex H: Risk = 1 + Impact x Feasibility.</em></li><li> According to the computed risk value,<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="f77ed7e0-6738-4276-95e2-b33854c5d6e2"> decide</ac:inline-comment-marker> the risk treatment for each threat scenario: Retain, Reduce, Share, or Avoid. Depending on the risk treatment decision, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b18dd0f5-50da-48e5-a536-d3e96f0b1e8c">determine</ac:inline-comment-marker> cybersecurity claims or goals that will eventually be detailed in the cybersecurity requirements (functional, technical, hardware, or software type).</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="faa93582-b57b-4b49-bd25-28be3230adea"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">An Item is a part of the system architecture to be protected. An Item with a Functional Cybersecurity Concept (output of the study) is the system architecture with additional requirements and claims that <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="eb7839d2-148c-42c6-9a64-784c8e8da763">ensures</ac:inline-comment-marker> a secure system.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249574156 space=SCD version=2 -->
## PAGE 00131: ISO 21434 Functional Cybersecurity

- page_id: `249574156`
- space_key: `SCD`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SCD/pages/249574156/ISO+21434+Functional+Cybersecurity
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T10:51:49.504+02:00`
- ancestors: Systems Cybersecurity Designer
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cybersecurity engineering is a critical discipline to ensure safe and secure human experiences using cyber physical systems. The main challenge is to preserve trust for connected cyber systems in an evolving digital economy increasingly threatened by unpredictable events.

Systems Cybersecurity Designer allows you to identify assets to be protected, threats and attacks to be tackled, and cybersecurity requirements to mitigate identified risks. The Systems Cybersecurity Designer supports the[ISO/SAE 21434 standard](https://www.iso.org/standard/70918.html). Itenables a proactive and continuous security assessment in the initial design phase to reduce product development cost and time. Systems cybersecurity designers are able to create a Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) project template to comply with the ISO/SAE 21434:2021.

The****ISO/SAE 21434:2021 standard specifies engineering requirements for cybersecurity risk management regarding the Concept, Product development, Production, Operations, and maintenance and decommissioning of electrical and electronic (E/E) systems in road vehicles. This standard includes the integral components and interfaces of the road vehicles. A framework includes requirements for cybersecurity processes and a common language for communicating and managing cybersecurity risk. It is applicable to electrical and electronic (E/E) systems of production road vehicles whose development or modification began after the publication of ISO/SAE 21434:2021. ISO/SAE 21434:2021 does not prescribe specific technology or solutions related to cybersecurity.

The Systems Cybersecurity Designerallows a cybersecurity designer to:

- Design a safe and secure system through a built-in scalable cyber system model, which includes assets, weaknesses, threats, attack paths, and security requirements.
- Perform continuous threat assessment and hazard analyses to enhance design through real world scenarios.
- Support certification needs with consistent safety and cybersecurity compliance views.

The Systems Cybersecurity Designerdirectly covers the following parts of the standard:

**9. Concept**

This chapter specifies the item with its cybersecurity goals and claims. It is composed of cybersecurity requirements and the operational environment requirements of an item.

**15. Threat analysis and risk assessment methods**

This chapter specifies the methods to determine the extent to which a threat scenario can impact a road user. These methods and their work products are known as Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment (TARA) and are performed from the viewpoint of affected road users. The TARA steps are generic modules that can be invoked systematically from any point in the lifecycle of an item or component.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Overview of ISO/SAE 21434:2021 standard

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

To install and use the Systems Cybersecurity Designer, ensure that one of the following modeling tools is installed:

- Magic Cyber Systems Engineer
- Magic Cyber Systems of Systems Architect
- Cameo Systems Modeler - Architect Edition
- Cameo Systems Modeler - Enterprise Edition
- Cameo Enterprise Architecture
- Magic Draw (any version) with SysML plugin installed

To learn more about how to download the installation files, refer [Downloading installation files](https://docs.nomagic.com/IL/?contextKey=downloading-installation-files&version=latest).

To install and use the Systems Cybersecurity Designer, ensure that the following plugins are installed in your modeling tool:

- Cameo Safety and Reliability Analyzer
- ISO 26262 plugin

The cybersecurity plugin is compliant to [RAAML standard](https://www.omg.org/spec/RAAML/1.0/Beta2/About-RAAML).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
